1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
153

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128	
129	Before stopping:
130	
131	1. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with: current result, lemmas discovered, concrete next step, dead-end warnings.
132	2. Save all changes; ensure the file compiles (use scoped `sorry` for stuck parts if needed).
133

6	import AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.LineBundlePullback
7	
8	/-!
9	# Coherence of locally trivial line bundles (A.2.c engine)
10	
11	This file is the **iter-256 Lane engine** file-skeleton for the cheap, on-path
12	half of the line-bundle coherence requirement of the A.2.c Quot-scheme
13	embedding (`chap:Picard_QuotScheme`, `chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability`).
14	
15	The Quot functor takes coherent quotients of a coherent sheaf; to feed it a line
16	bundle one must know that the line bundle is itself coherent, i.e. a finitely
17	presented `𝒪`-module. The embedding's input is a point of the relative Picard
18	scheme `Pic⁰_{C/k}`, whose carrier is the **locally trivial** predicate
19	`IsLocallyTrivial` of `chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback` (the project-side stand-in
20	for an invertible sheaf). The chapter proves
21	`IsLocallyTrivial M ⟹ M.IsFinitePresentation`, together with a rank-one /
22	flatness record. The general implication "`M` tensor-invertible `⟹` `M` locally
23	free of rank one" (the converse spreading-out, Stacks Lemma 0B8M when stalks are
24	local) is *not* crossed here: the carrier is already locally trivial, so the
25	finite-presentation spreading-out is never needed.
26	
27	## Status (iter-257 bodies opened: 5 sorry → 1)
28	
29	The engine is reduced to a **single** missing ingredient. Of the five pinned
30	declarations, four are closed axiom-clean and the fifth (`chartPresentation`) is
31	complete modulo one isolated isomorphism `chartOverIso` (the only remaining
32	`sorry`, line ~178):
33	
34	* `exists_trivializing_cover` — CLOSED (axiom-clean): repackage the pointwise
35	  `IsLocallyTrivial` existential as an indexed affine cover.
36	* `chart_free_rank_one` — CLOSED (axiom-clean): the chart-local trivialisation.
37	* `chartPresentation` — built as `unitPresentation.ofIsIso chartOverIso.inv`; its
38	  `IsFinite` rides on the `ofIsIso` instance. Reduces to `chartOverIso`.
39	* `isFinitePresentation` — CLOSED modulo `chartOverIso`: assembles the
40	  `QuasicoherentData` from the cover + `chartPresentation`, `shrink`s the index
41	  into the site-object universe, and feeds `IsFinitePresentation.mk`.
42	* `isFiniteType` — CLOSED modulo `chartOverIso`: the Mathlib
43	  `IsFinitePresentation → IsFiniteType` instance.
44	
45	Two reusable axiom-clean bricks were added: `freeUnitIso` (`free PUnit ≅ unit R`)
46	and `unitPresentation` (the canonical finite presentation of the unit module,
47	one generator / no relations), with a `Presentation.IsFinite` instance.
48	
49	**The one open `sorry` — `chartOverIso`** — is the over↔restrict trivialisation
50	bridge: an isomorphism `M.over U ≅ unit (X.ringCatSheaf.over U)` transporting the
51	given scheme-level trivialisation `e : M.restrict U.ι ≅ unit (↑U).ringCatSheaf`.
52	The two source categories are equivalent via the open-immersion site equivalence
53	`Opens.overEquivalence U : Over U ≌ Opens ↥U`
54	(`Mathlib.Topology.Sheaves.Over`), but lifting that equivalence to the level of
55	*sheaves of modules* (the modules-level shadow of `overSliceSheafEquiv`,
56	identifying `restrict ↦ over` and `unit ↦ unit`) is not yet available in the
57	project and is Mathlib-scale; it is the same wall blocking the dual lane. See
58	`informal/chartOverIso.md`.
59	
60	The 5 pinned declarations are:
61	
62	1. `IsLocallyTrivial.exists_trivializing_cover` (`lem:lbc_trivializing_cover`) —
63	   repackage the pointwise existential of `IsLocallyTrivial` into an indexed
64	   trivialising affine cover.
65	2. `IsLocallyTrivial.chartPresentation` (`lem:lbc_chart_presentation`) — the
66	   trivial finite free presentation of `M.over U` on a trivialising chart.
67	3. `IsLocallyTrivial.isFinitePresentation` (`thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation`) —
68	   the main theorem: assemble a `QuasicoherentData` from the trivialising cover
69	   and feed it to `SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation.mk`.
70	4. `IsLocallyTrivial.isFiniteType` (`cor:lbc_isFiniteType`) — finite type +
71	   quasi-coherence, immediate from finite presentation.
72	5. `IsLocallyTrivial.chart_free_rank_one` (`lem:lbc_rank_flat`) — the chart-local
73	   rank-one free record.
74	
75	## Site instances (the chief de-risk; see `rem:lbc_site_instances`)
76	
77	Mathlib's `SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation` / `QuasicoherentData` are stated
78	under three site hypotheses on the slice topologies `J.over U` of the underlying
79	topology `J = Opens.grothendieckTopology X` of `X.ringCatSheaf`:
80	
81	```
82	[∀ U, (J.over U).HasSheafCompose (forget₂ RingCat AddCommGrpCat)]
83	[∀ U, HasWeakSheafify (J.over U) AddCommGrpCat]     -- (resp. HasSheafify)
84	[∀ U, (J.over U).WEqualsLocallyBijective AddCommGrpCat]
85	```
86	
87	The de-risk outcome (whether these resolve for `X.ringCatSheaf`) is recorded in
88	the task result and probed by the `#check` commands at the end of this file.

94	(Tag 01CS, invertible modules), "Modules of finite presentation", and
95	Lemma 17.25.4 (Tag 0B8M).

112	/-! ## §1. From pointwise triviality to a trivialising cover -/
113	
114	/-- **Trivialising affine cover** (Stacks 01CS / Definition 17.25.1).
115	A locally trivial module `M` admits an indexed trivialising affine cover: an
116	index type `I`, a family of affine opens `U i` covering `X`, and for each `i` a
117	`𝒪_{U_i}`-module isomorphism `M|_{U_i} ≅ 𝒪_{U_i}`.
118	
119	The witness repackages the pointwise existential `IsLocallyTrivial M` (take
120	`I := X` and assign each point its chosen neighbourhood). -/
121	theorem IsLocallyTrivial.exists_trivializing_cover {M : X.Modules}
122	    (hM : IsLocallyTrivial M) :
123	    ∃ (I : Type u) (U : I → X.Opens),
124	      (∀ i, IsAffineOpen (U i)) ∧ iSup U = ⊤ ∧
125	        ∀ i, Nonempty (M.restrict (U i).ι ≅
126	          SheafOfModules.unit (U i : Scheme).ringCatSheaf) := by
127	  refine ⟨X, fun x => (hM x).choose, fun x => (hM x).choose_spec.2.1, ?_,
128	    fun x => (hM x).choose_spec.2.2⟩
129	  rw [eq_top_iff]
130	  intro x _
131	  rw [TopologicalSpace.Opens.mem_iSup]
132	  exact ⟨x, (hM x).choose_spec.1⟩
133	
134	/-! ## §1b. The canonical finite presentation of the unit module -/
135	
136	section UnitPresentation
137	
138	universe u₁ v₁
139	
140	variable {C : Type u₁} [Category.{v₁} C] {J : GrothendieckTopology C}
141	  {R : Sheaf J RingCat.{u}}
142	  [HasSheafify J AddCommGrpCat.{u}] [J.WEqualsLocallyBijective AddCommGrpCat.{u}]
143	  [J.HasSheafCompose (forget₂ RingCat.{u} AddCommGrpCat.{u})]
144	
145	/-- The canonical isomorphism `free PUnit ≅ unit R`: the free module on a single
146	generator is the unit module. -/
147	noncomputable def freeUnitIso :
148	    SheafOfModules.free.{u} (R := R) PUnit.{u + 1} ≅ SheafOfModules.unit R :=
149	  Limits.coproductUniqueIso (fun (_ : PUnit.{u + 1}) ↦ SheafOfModules.unit R)
150	
151	/-- The canonical generating sections of `unit R`: one generator (the image of
152	the free generator under `freeUnitIso`). -/
153	noncomputable def unitGenerators : (SheafOfModules.unit R).GeneratingSections where
154	  I := PUnit.{u + 1}
155	  s := (SheafOfModules.unit R).freeHomEquiv freeUnitIso.hom
156	  epi := by
157	    rw [Equiv.symm_apply_apply]
158	    infer_instance
159	
160	/-- The canonical finite presentation of the unit module `unit R`: one generator,
161	no relations (its generating map is an isomorphism, so the relation kernel is the
162	zero object). -/
163	noncomputable def unitPresentation : (SheafOfModules.unit R).Presentation where
164	  generators := unitGenerators
165	  relations :=
166	    { I := PEmpty.{u + 1}
167	      s := PEmpty.elim
168	      epi := by
169	        have hiso : IsIso (unitGenerators (R := R)).π := by
170	          dsimp only [unitGenerators, SheafOfModules.GeneratingSections.π]
171	          rw [Equiv.symm_apply_apply]
172	          infer_instance
173	        have : Mono (unitGenerators (R := R)).π := inferInstance
174	        have hz : Limits.IsZero (Limits.kernel (unitGenerators (R := R)).π) :=
175	          Limits.isZero_kernel_of_mono _
176	        exact ⟨fun g h _ => hz.eq_of_src g h⟩ }
177	
178	instance : (unitPresentation (R := R)).IsFinite where
179	  isFiniteType_generators := ⟨inferInstanceAs (Finite PUnit.{u + 1})⟩
180	  isFiniteType_relations := ⟨inferInstanceAs (Finite PEmpty.{u + 1})⟩
181	
182	end UnitPresentation
183	
184	/-! ## §2. The trivial presentation on a chart -/
185	
186	/-- **The over↔restrict trivialisation bridge.** Transports the scheme-level
187	trivialisation `e : M.restrict U.ι ≅ 𝒪_U` (an isomorphism in
188	`SheafOfModules (↑U).ringCatSheaf`, the category of modules on the *open
189	subscheme* `U`) to a slice-level trivialisation
190	`M.over U ≅ 𝒪_U` (in `SheafOfModules (X.ringCatSheaf.over U)`, the category of
191	modules on the *slice site* `J.over U`).
192	
193	The two source categories are equivalent — the open-immersion functor
194	`U.ι.opensFunctor : Opens ↥U ≌ {V ∈ Opens X | V ≤ U} = Over U` induces an
195	equivalence `SheafOfModules (↑U).ringCatSheaf ≌ SheafOfModules (X.ringCatSheaf.over U)`
196	identifying `M.restrict U.ι ↦ M.over U` and `unit ↦ unit` — but this
197	equivalence of *sheaf-of-modules* categories (the modules-level shadow of
198	`Opens.overEquivalence` / `overSliceSheafEquiv`) is not yet available in the
199	project, so the bridge is recorded here as a typed `sorry`. This is the single
200	missing ingredient for the whole `LineBundleCoherence` engine: everything else
201	(`chartPresentation`, `isFinitePresentation`, `isFiniteType`) is complete modulo
202	this isomorphism. -/
203	noncomputable def chartOverIso (M : X.Modules) (U : X.Opens)
204	    (e : M.restrict U.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (U : Scheme).ringCatSheaf) :
205	    M.over U ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (X.ringCatSheaf.over U) :=
206	  sorry
207	
208	/-- **Finite free presentation on a chart** (Stacks, "Modules of finite
209	presentation"). On a trivialising chart `U` — i.e. with an isomorphism
210	`M|_U ≅ 𝒪_U` — the restriction `M.over U` admits the trivial finite free
211	presentation (one generator, no relations), obtained by transporting the
212	canonical presentation of `SheafOfModules.unit` along the trivialisation
213	(through the `chartOverIso` bridge). -/
214	noncomputable def IsLocallyTrivial.chartPresentation (M : X.Modules) (U : X.Opens)
215	    (e : M.restrict U.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (U : Scheme).ringCatSheaf) :
216	    (M.over U).Presentation := by
217	  exact SheafOfModules.Presentation.ofIsIso (R := X.ringCatSheaf.over U)
218	    (M := SheafOfModules.unit (X.ringCatSheaf.over U)) (N := SheafOfModules.over M U)
219	    (chartOverIso M U e).inv (unitPresentation (R := X.ringCatSheaf.over U))
220	
221	instance (M : X.Modules) (U : X.Opens)
222	    (e : M.restrict U.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (U : Scheme).ringCatSheaf) :
223	    (IsLocallyTrivial.chartPresentation M U e).IsFinite := by
224	  dsimp only [IsLocallyTrivial.chartPresentation]
225	  infer_instance
226	
227	/-! ## §3. Finite presentation of a locally trivial line bundle -/
228	
229	/-- **Locally trivial `⟹` finitely presented** (Stacks 0B8M / Lemma 17.25.4).
230	A locally trivial module on a scheme is finitely presented: assemble a
231	`QuasicoherentData` from the trivialising affine cover
232	(`exists_trivializing_cover`), whose presentation at each index is the finite
233	free presentation of `chartPresentation`, and feed it to
234	`SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation.mk`. No stalk computation or
235	neighbourhood-spreading is involved. -/
236	theorem IsLocallyTrivial.isFinitePresentation {M : X.Modules}
237	    (hM : IsLocallyTrivial M) :
238	    M.IsFinitePresentation := by
239	  obtain ⟨I, U, hUaff, hUtop, hUiso⟩ := hM.exists_trivializing_cover
240	  -- assemble the quasi-coherent data, one finite chart presentation per index;
241	  -- `shrink` lands the index type in the site-object universe demanded by
242	  -- `IsFinitePresentation`'s existential.
243	  let q : M.QuasicoherentData :=
244	    { I := I
245	      X := U
246	      coversTop := by
247	        -- the trivialising affine opens cover the terminal object of the site
248	        intro W x hxW
249	        have hx : x ∈ iSup U := by rw [hUtop]; trivial
250	        rw [TopologicalSpace.Opens.mem_iSup] at hx
251	        obtain ⟨i, hi⟩ := hx
252	        exact ⟨U i ⊓ W, homOfLE inf_le_right, ⟨i, ⟨homOfLE inf_le_left⟩⟩, hi, hxW⟩
253	      presentation := fun i => IsLocallyTrivial.chartPresentation M (U i) (hUiso i).some }
254	  have hsh : q.shrink.IsFinitePresentation := by
255	    apply SheafOfModules.QuasicoherentData.IsFinitePresentation.mk
256	    intro i
257	    exact inferInstanceAs (IsLocallyTrivial.chartPresentation M _ _).IsFinite
258	  exact { exists_quasicoherentData := ⟨q.shrink, hsh⟩ }
259	
260	/-- **Locally trivial `⟹` finite type and quasi-coherent** (corollary of
261	`isFinitePresentation`). Finite presentation implies finite type by Mathlib's
262	`SheafOfModules.instIsFiniteTypeOfIsFinitePresentation` and quasi-coherence by
263	`SheafOfModules.instIsQuasicoherentOfIsFinitePresentation`. -/
264	theorem IsLocallyTrivial.isFiniteType {M : X.Modules}
265	    (hM : IsLocallyTrivial M) :
266	    M.IsFiniteType := by
267	  haveI := hM.isFinitePresentation
268	  infer_instance
269	
270	/-! ## §4. Rank one and flatness record -/
271	
272	/-- **Chart-local rank one and flatness** (Stacks, "Locally free sheaves":
273	finite locally free of rank `r`). On a trivialising chart the restriction
274	`M|_U ≅ 𝒪_U` is free of rank one over `𝒪_U`; in particular flat (`𝒪_U` is a flat
275	module over itself, and flatness is preserved by the isomorphism). The fact is
276	recorded chart-locally — as the rank-one free model `M|_U ≅ 𝒪_U = ⊕_{*} 𝒪_U`
277	the Quot embedding consumes — because Mathlib has no `SheafOfModules`-level
278	locally-free / flat / rank predicate. -/
279	theorem IsLocallyTrivial.chart_free_rank_one {M : X.Modules}
280	    (hM : IsLocallyTrivial M) (x : X) :
281	    ∃ U : X.Opens, x ∈ U ∧ IsAffineOpen U ∧
282	      Nonempty (M.restrict U.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (U : Scheme).ringCatSheaf) := by
283	  exact hM x
284	
285	end LineBundle
286	
287	end Scheme
288	
289	end AlgebraicGeometry

1	\chapter{Coherence of locally trivial line bundles}
2	\label{chap:Picard_LineBundleCoherence}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundleCoherence.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter supplies the cheap, on-path half of the
6	% line-bundle coherence requirement of the A.2.c Quot-scheme embedding
7	% (\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}).
8	% The Quot functor takes coherent quotients of a coherent sheaf; to feed it a
9	% line bundle one must know that the line bundle is itself coherent, i.e. a
10	% finitely presented \(\mathcal{O}\)-module. The embedding's input is a point of
11	% the relative Picard scheme \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\), whose carrier is the
12	% \emph{locally trivial} predicate \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) of
13	% \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (the project-side stand-in for an
14	% invertible sheaf, a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules locally isomorphic to
15	% \(\mathcal{O}\) of rank one). The chapter proves
16	% \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial} M \Rightarrow M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\),
17	% together with a rank-one / flatness record. The general implication
18	% ``\(M\) tensor-invertible \(\Rightarrow\) \(M\) locally free of rank one''
19	% (the converse spreading-out, Stacks Lemma~0B8M when stalks are local) is
20	% \emph{not} crossed here: the carrier is already locally trivial, so the
21	% finite-presentation spreading-out is never needed.
22	
23	\section{Setup and motivation}
24	\label{sec:lbc_setup}
25	
26	Fix a scheme \(X\) and write \(X.\mathtt{Modules}\) for the category of sheaves
27	of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (Mathlib's \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) on the
28	ringed-space site of \(X\), with structure sheaf \(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)).
29	A \emph{line bundle} on \(X\) is an invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module; in the
30	sense of \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} we model line bundles by the
31	predicate \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}\): a module \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) is locally
32	trivial of rank one if every point \(x \in X\) has an \emph{affine} open
33	neighbourhood \(U\) on which the restriction \(M|_U\) is isomorphic, as an
34	\(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module, to the structure sheaf-as-module \(\struct{U}\):
35	\[
36	  \texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M) \;:=\;
37	  \forall x \in X,\ \exists\, U \ni x \text{ affine open},\quad
38	  M|_U \;\cong\; \struct{U}.
39	\]
40	This is the rank-one case of Stacks Definition~17.25.1
41	(Tag~01CS, invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules), and
42	agrees with the classical ``locally free of rank one'' description by Stacks
43	Lemma~17.25.4 (Tag~0B8M).
44	
45	The goal of the chapter is to record that such a module is \emph{coherent} in the
46	sense the Quot construction consumes, namely finitely presented:
47	\(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\) in Mathlib's
48	\(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation}\) (built from a
49	\(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\)). The mathematics is elementary: on each
50	trivialising chart \(M|_U \cong \struct{U}\) is finite free of rank one, hence
51	has the trivial finite presentation, and finite presentation is by definition a
52	local condition. No stalk computation and no spreading-out are involved. The
53	content is the bookkeeping that turns the pointwise trivialisation into the
54	indexed \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\) Mathlib expects.
55	
56	\section{Site instances for quasi-coherent data}
57	\label{sec:lbc_site_instances}
58	
59	% NOTE (chief unknown). Mathlib's SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation /
60	% QuasicoherentData are stated under three site hypotheses on the slice
61	% topologies J.over X of the underlying site J of X.ringCatSheaf:
62	%   [∀ x, (J.over x).HasSheafCompose (forget₂ RingCat AddCommGrpCat)]
63	%   [∀ x, HasWeakSheafify (J.over x) AddCommGrpCat]
64	%   [∀ x, (J.over x).WEqualsLocallyBijective AddCommGrpCat]
65	% These must be discharged (or assumed as instances) before the statements in
66	% this chapter typecheck for X.ringCatSheaf. The project already lives in the
67	% SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf world, so these should resolve; this is the one
68	% item to verify when the Lean file is scaffolded.
69	
70	The Mathlib predicates \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation}\) and
71	\(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.QuasicoherentData}\) are stated relative to a
72	Grothendieck topology \(J\) on a base category \(C\) and a sheaf of rings
73	\(R : \Sheaf(J, \RingCat)\). They require, for every object \(x : C\), three
74	instances on the slice topology \(J/x\) (Mathlib \(J.\mathtt{over}\ x\)):
75	\[
76	  (J/x).\mathtt{HasSheafCompose}(\forget_{\RingCat \to \AddCommGrpCat}),\quad
77	  \HasWeakSheafify(J/x, \AddCommGrpCat),\quad
78	  (J/x).\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}(\AddCommGrpCat).
79	\]
80	For \(X.\mathtt{Modules} = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\ X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)
81	these are the ambient site instances of the ringed space underlying \(X\). We
82	record their availability as a standing assumption for the chapter; verifying it
83	for \(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is the single instance-resolution check the
84	formalisation must clear at entry, and is independent of the mathematical
85	content below.
86	
87	\begin{remark}
88	  \label{rem:lbc_site_instances}
89	  For the ringed-space site underlying \(X\) with sheaf of rings
90	  \(R = X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\), every slice topology \(J/x\) carries the
91	  three instances \(\mathtt{HasSheafCompose}\), \(\HasWeakSheafify\) and
92	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) (for the forgetful functor
93	  \(\RingCat \to \AddCommGrpCat\)) demanded by
94	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.QuasicoherentData}\). Under this assumption the
95	  predicate \(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\) is well-formed for every
96	  \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\).
97	\end{remark}
98	
99	\section{From pointwise triviality to a trivialising cover}
100	\label{sec:lbc_cover}
101	
102	\begin{lemma}\leanok
103	[Trivialising affine cover]
104	  \label{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
105	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.exists_trivializing_cover}
106	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product}
107	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Definition 17.25.1
108	  % "invertible module" (Tag 01CS), Section 17.25 (Tag 01CR)
109	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4046-L4059).
110	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
111	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. An
112	  %  {\it invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module} is a sheaf
113	  %  of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{L}$ such that
114	  %  the functor
115	  %  $$
116	  %  \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \longrightarrow \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X),\quad
117	  %  \mathcal{F} \longmapsto \mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{F}
118	  %  $$
119	  %  is an equivalence of categories. We say that $\mathcal{L}$ is
120	  %  {\it trivial} if it is isomorphic as an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
121	  %  to $\mathcal{O}_X$."
122	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~01CS (Modules, Definition~17.25.1).}
123	  Let \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) be locally trivial, \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M)\).
124	  Then there exist an index set \(I\), a family of affine open subschemes
125	  \(\{U_i\}_{i \in I}\) of \(X\) covering \(X\) (i.e. \(\bigcup_i U_i = X\)), and
126	  for each \(i\) an isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules
127	  \[
128	    e_i \;:\; M|_{U_i} \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \struct{U_i}
129	  \]
130	  to the structure sheaf-as-module on \(U_i\).
131	\end{lemma}
132	
133	\begin{proof}
134	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product}
135	  Apply the hypothesis \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M)\) at every point \(x \in X\): it
136	  produces an affine open \(U_x \ni x\) and an isomorphism
137	  \(e_x : M|_{U_x} \cong \struct{U_x}\). Take the index set \(I := X\) (the
138	  underlying set of points), and to the index \(x\) assign the chosen
139	  neighbourhood \(U_x\) and the chosen isomorphism \(e_x\). The family
140	  \(\{U_x\}_{x \in X}\) covers \(X\) because each \(x\) lies in its own \(U_x\).
141	  This is exactly the data the definition supplies, packaged as an indexed
142	  family rather than a pointwise existential.
143	\end{proof}
144	
145	\section{The trivial presentation on a chart}
146	\label{sec:lbc_chart_presentation}
147	
148	\begin{lemma}\leanok
149	[Finite free presentation on a chart]
150	  \label{lem:lbc_chart_presentation}
151	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.chartPresentation}
152	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
153	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, section
154	  % "Modules of finite presentation" (\label{definition-finite-presentation})
155	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L1377-L1392).
156	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
157	  % "We say that $\mathcal{F}$ is of {\it finite presentation}
158	  %  if for every point $x \in X$ there exists an open neighbourhood
159	  %  $x\in U \subset X$, and  $n, m \in \mathbf{N}$ such that $\mathcal{F}|_U$
160	  %  is isomorphic to the cokernel of a map
161	  %  $$
162	  %  \bigoplus\nolimits_{j = 1, \ldots, m}
163	  %  \mathcal{O}_U
164	  %  \longrightarrow
165	  %  \bigoplus\nolimits_{i = 1, \ldots, n}
166	  %  \mathcal{O}_U
167	  %  $$"
168	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules, ``Modules of finite
169	  presentation''.}
170	  On each chart \(U_i\) of the trivialising cover of
171	  \cref{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}, the restriction \(M|_{U_i}\) admits the
172	  trivial finite free presentation
173	  \[
174	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \struct{U_i} \;\longrightarrow\; M|_{U_i}
175	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0,
176	  \]
177	  i.e. \(M|_{U_i}\) is the cokernel of the zero map
178	  \(\bigoplus_{j \in \varnothing} \struct{U_i} \to \struct{U_i}\)
179	  (the case \(m = 0\), \(n = 1\) of the finite-presentation pattern). The
180	  unit sheaf-as-module \(\struct{U_i}\) (Mathlib's \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\))
181	  carries a canonical free presentation on one generator with no relations;
182	  this presentation is finite.
183	\end{lemma}
184	
185	\begin{proof}
186	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
187	  The structure sheaf-as-module \(\struct{U_i} = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,
188	  (U_i).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is free of rank one on the single generator
189	  \(1\): the identity map \(\struct{U_i} \to \struct{U_i}\) exhibits it as
190	  \(\bigoplus_{i \in \{*\}} \struct{U_i}\), so the surjection
191	  \(\struct{U_i}^{\oplus 1} \twoheadrightarrow \struct{U_i}\) is an isomorphism
192	  and its kernel is zero, generated by the empty family. Thus \(\struct{U_i}\)
193	  has the canonical presentation with \(n = 1\) generators and \(m = 0\)
194	  relations; this canonical \(\mathtt{unit}\)-presentation is the rank-one free
195	  presentation, and Mathlib records it as \emph{finite} (the
196	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\) finite-presentation instance).
197	
198	  The chart presentation is obtained by transporting the canonical finite
199	  presentation of \(\mathtt{unit}\,(U_i).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) along the
200	  isomorphism \(e_i\), using \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.Presentation.ofIsIso}\).
201	  This construction carries any finite presentation of a module to a finite
202	  presentation of any isomorphic module, so the transported presentation of
203	  \(M|_{U_i}\) is again finite.
204	  % NOTE (review iter-257): this sketch elides the over<->restrict CATEGORICAL
205	  % BRIDGE. The given e_i lives in SheafOfModules (U_i:Scheme).ringCatSheaf
206	  % (open-subscheme site), whereas Presentation.ofIsIso must act in
207	  % SheafOfModules (X.ringCatSheaf.over U_i) (slice site J.over U_i) -- DIFFERENT
208	  % categories. The Lean bridges them through an iso `chartOverIso`
209	  % (M.over U ≅ unit (X.ringCatSheaf.over U)), which is the SOLE remaining
210	  % sorry of this file and is Mathlib-scale: the modules-level lift of
211	  % `Opens.overEquivalence`. THIS IS THE SAME WALL as the dual lane
212	  % (`exists_tensorObj_inverse`/`dual_restrict_iso`). A prover following this
213	  % sketch verbatim hits an immediate type mismatch. Blueprint-writer must add a
214	  % block for the bridge; see informal/chartOverIso.md and
215	  % task_results/lean-vs-blueprint-checker-lvb-lbc257.md.
216	\end{proof}

218	\section{Finite presentation of a locally trivial line bundle}
219	\label{sec:lbc_finite_presentation}
220	
221	\begin{theorem}\leanok
222	[Locally trivial \(\Rightarrow\) finitely presented]
223	  \label{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}
224	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.isFinitePresentation}
225	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover, lem:lbc_chart_presentation, rem:lbc_site_instances}
226	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces,
227	  % Lemma 17.25.4 (Tag 0B8M), invertible vs locally free of rank one
228	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4159-L4165).
229	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
230	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Any locally free
231	  %  $\mathcal{O}_X$-module of rank $1$ is invertible.
232	  %  If all stalks $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ are local rings, then
233	  %  the converse holds as well (but in general this is not the case)."
234	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~0B8M (Modules, Lemma~17.25.4).}
235	  Let \(X\) be a scheme satisfying \cref{rem:lbc_site_instances} and let
236	  \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) be locally trivial, \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M)\). Then
237	  \(M\) is finitely presented: \(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\) holds in
238	  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation}\).
239	\end{theorem}
240	
241	\begin{proof}
242	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover, lem:lbc_chart_presentation}
243	  By \cref{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover} choose a trivialising affine cover
244	  \(\{U_i\}_{i \in I}\) with isomorphisms \(e_i : M|_{U_i} \cong \struct{U_i}\).
245	  Assemble these into a \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\) for \(M\): the index type
246	  is \(I\), the covering objects are the affine opens \(U_i\) (which cover \(X\)),
247	  and the presentation attached to index \(i\) is the finite free presentation of
248	  \(M|_{U_i}\) (equivalently, of the restriction \(M.\mathtt{over}\,U_i\))
249	  produced in \cref{lem:lbc_chart_presentation}. Each such presentation is
250	  finite by \cref{lem:lbc_chart_presentation}; the assembled
251	  \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\) therefore satisfies
252	  \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData.IsFinitePresentation}\), and the constructor
253	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation.mk}\) yields
254	  \(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\).
255	
256	  The only non-formal input is the trivialising cover; everything else is the
257	  definitional unwinding of finite presentation as a local property
258	  (Stacks, ``Modules of finite presentation''), since a rank-one free module is its own finite
259	  presentation. No stalk computation and no neighbourhood-spreading argument is
260	  used: the hypothesis already provides the local free models.
261	\end{proof}
262	
263	\begin{corollary}\leanok
264	[Locally trivial \(\Rightarrow\) finite type]
265	  \label{cor:lbc_isFiniteType}
266	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.isFiniteType}
267	  \uses{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}
268	  Under the hypotheses of \cref{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}, the locally
269	  trivial module \(M\) is of finite type (\(M.\mathtt{IsFiniteType}\)). Quasi-coherence
270	  (\(M.\mathtt{IsQuasicoherent}\)) follows from finite presentation via
271	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.instIsQuasicoherentOfIsFinitePresentation}\).
272	\end{corollary}
273	
274	\begin{proof}
275	  \uses{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}
276	  Finite presentation implies finite type by Mathlib's
277	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.instIsFiniteTypeOfIsFinitePresentation}\), immediate
278	  from \cref{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}. Quasi-coherence is immediate from
279	  finite presentation by
280	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.instIsQuasicoherentOfIsFinitePresentation}\).
281	\end{proof}

283	\section{Rank one and flatness record}
284	\label{sec:lbc_rank_flat}
285	
286	% NOTE. Mathlib (at the pin) has no SheafOfModules-level "locally free of rank r"
287	% or "flat" predicate (verified absent: no SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFree). The
288	% rank-one and flatness facts are therefore recorded chart-locally, as the data
289	% the Quot embedding actually consumes, rather than as instances of a missing
290	% global predicate. Project-side predicates may be introduced if a downstream
291	% consumer needs them packaged; this is flagged for the plan agent.
292	
293	\begin{lemma}\leanok
294	[Chart-local rank one and flatness]
295	  \label{lem:lbc_rank_flat}
296	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.chart_free_rank_one}
297	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
298	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, section
299	  % "Locally free sheaves" (\label{definition-locally-free}, "(finite)
300	  % locally free of rank $r$")
301	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L2079-L2094).
302	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
303	  % "\item We say $\mathcal{F}$ is {\it locally free} if for every
304	  %  point $x \in X$ there exist a set $I$ and an open
305	  %  neighbourhood $x \in U \subset X$
306	  %  such that $\mathcal{F}|_U$ is isomorphic to
307	  %  $\bigoplus_{i \in I} \mathcal{O}_X|_U$ as an $\mathcal{O}_X|_U$-module.
308	  %  \item We say $\mathcal{F}$ is {\it finite locally free} if we may
309	  %  choose the index sets $I$ to be finite.
310	  %  \item We say $\mathcal{F}$ is {\it finite locally free of rank $r$}
311	  %  if we may choose the index sets $I$ to have cardinality $r$."
312	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules, ``Locally free sheaves'' (finite
313	  locally free of rank \(r\)).}
314	  Let \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) be locally trivial. The lemma records, at each
315	  point \(x \in X\), a chart \(U \ni x\) together with the trivialising iso
316	  \(M|_U \cong \struct{U}\) (to \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\)). This iso is the
317	  rank-one free \emph{model}: rank-one freeness and flatness are
318	  \emph{consequences} of this iso, because \(\struct{U}\) is the free
319	  \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module of rank one and a flat module over itself (a ring is
320	  flat over itself), and both properties transport along \(e\). In the sense of
321	  \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product} this exhibits \(M\) as finite locally free
322	  of rank one and flat on the cover.
323	\end{lemma}
324	
325	\begin{proof}
326	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
327	  Immediate from the trivialisation: on \(U_i\), \(e_i\) identifies \(M|_{U_i}\)
328	  with \(\struct{U_i} = \bigoplus_{i \in \{*\}} \struct{U_i}\), the free module
329	  of rank one. The structure sheaf-as-module is flat over itself, and an
330	  isomorphic module is flat; the index set \(\{*\}\) has cardinality one, so the
331	  rank is one. The properties are recorded chart-locally because rank-one
332	  freeness and flatness are local conditions, and the chart-local data are what
333	  the Quot-scheme embedding consumes when it forms the Hilbert-polynomial input
334	  on a fibre.
335	\end{proof}

Read `archon-protected.yaml` before touching any declaration. You may fill proof bodies of protected declarations but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.

## Avoid early termination

- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
- **Decompose**: break into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available) and solve each individually.
- **Hard bar is a minimum, not a ceiling.** If your objectives specify a "hard bar" (e.g. "add def + pin signature"), that tells you the minimum required — not where to stop. After meeting it:
  - If a recipe exists in `analogies/`, the blueprint chapter has a concrete proof sketch, or you can reason about an approach: attempt the proof body immediately.
  - If no recipe exists: formulate a strategy, attempt it, and leave a partial tactic block at the stuck point. A failed attempt with a `by ... sorry` is far more useful than a clean `sorry`.
  - A bare `sorry` with no attempt is only acceptable when the mathematical obstacle is explicitly named and at least one concrete approach has been tried and documented.
- **After closing all assigned sorries, keep going.** Scan your file for other open `sorry`s that share the mathematical context you've just developed. If you can see a direct path to any of them using lemmas or infrastructure you've already built, attempt them. Stopping at the assigned task boundary when adjacent work is tractable is artificial throttling — you have write permission over your whole file.
- **Comments are not progress. Code is.** If you can describe an approach precisely enough to write it as a comment or TODO, you can attempt it. Any `-- TODO: try X`, `-- could use Y here`, or `/- Next step: ... -/` you write is a sign you stopped too early — attempt X/Y first; only leave the comment if the attempt fails with a named error. The same applies to approaches proposed in task results: if you wrote it in your log as "a possible route", you should have tried it.
- **Autonomous decision-making within your file.** You are fully authorized to make every tactical and mathematical decision within your assigned `.lean` file without waiting for the planner. This includes: choosing proof strategies, adding helper lemmas, attempting adjacent sorries, selecting alternative approaches, deciding proof structure. **Never write "I'll let the planner decide", "I'll defer to the next iteration", or "this requires planner input" for any choice that is entirely within your file.** The planner is not on call; deferring wastes the entire context-loading investment of this session.

  The ONLY actions that require stopping rather than acting:
  - Modifying a *signature* of a protected declaration (you may still fill its proof body).
  - Editing another agent's `.lean` file.
  - Removing an existing public declaration that other files import.
  - Changing the type of an existing declaration in a way that would break other files.

  Everything else — including trying a strategy the planner didn't explicitly endorse — is your call to make and attempt now.

## When to stop

Stop only when progress is blocked by a specific, named obstacle in one of these categories:

1. A genuinely missing Mathlib ingredient — named precisely, attempted via informal agent, no workaround found.
2. A definition or signature that requires another prover's output — name the specific declaration and file.
3. A proof that requires fundamental restructuring of a protected or cross-file definition — name it.

"It seems hard", "the proof is complex", "I couldn't find the right lemma", or "the planner should weigh in on this approach" are NOT valid stop reasons. Document the specific obstacle and keep trying. Only stop when you can write: "I tried approaches X, Y, Z; each fails because [specific mathematical reason]; the informal agent suggested [route] which requires [specific missing ingredient]."

**Specifically banned stop phrases:**
- "I'll wait for the planner to decide."
- "This strategy should be confirmed by the next iteration."
- "I'll defer this choice to the planner."
- "The planner may want to reconsider the approach."

If you find yourself writing any of these: delete the sentence, pick the most promising route, and attempt it. The planner will correct the choice next iter if it was wrong — a failed attempt costs nothing compared to an idle session.

## Completion criteria

Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:

1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
2. Zero axioms introduced.
3. The file compiles cleanly.

## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof

When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature**. Three patterns are banned:

- **Reflexive-iso placeholder** — replacing `X ≅ Y` with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`.
- **`Classical.choice` around an explicit witness** — dissolving `Type` vs `Prop` friction without actually constructing the witness.
- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`** — discards the declaration post-elaboration, breaking blueprint cross-references.

**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, ship the typed `sorry` instead.

## When infrastructure is missing

Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before filing "Infrastructure missing":

1. **Use the informal agent** if an API key is available (check `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` first): call `.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py` with "Prove [goal] using only current Mathlib." Formalize whatever it suggests. If no key is set, skip to step 2.
2. **If the missing ingredient is ≤~100 LOC**: write it as a project-local helper — typed signature plus a genuine proof attempt. A partial proof body is better than a documented gap.
3. **Only after steps 1–2 are exhausted**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` with what you tried, why it failed, and the precise statement of the missing ingredient for the plan agent to assign in `mathlib-build` mode.

"Infrastructure missing" as a verdict requires: informal agent called (or key unavailable, documented), local helper attempted if in scope. "I don't see how" is not evidence — a failed attempt with `sorry` at the stuck step IS evidence.

When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.

**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.

## Proof style

- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is display-level only. Default to `change` when in doubt.

## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md

The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:

- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.

## LSP MCP tools

The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (`mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal`, `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`, etc.). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, …) — same tools, never shell binaries.

- Always invoke through your tool-call interface.
- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` — there is no such shell command.
- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If `success: false`, retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.

## Search protocol

1. `lean_local_search` first.
2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.

## Tooling traps

- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading. Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors. Rely on `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.

## Logging

Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):

```markdown
# Algebra/WLocal.lean

## wLocal_iff (line 45)
### Attempt 1
- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance
- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing

### Attempt 2
- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
- **Result:** RESOLVED
- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
```

One section per theorem/lemma. Each attempt: approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results.

## End-of-session handoff

**Before declaring done, run this self-review:**

1. Did I attempt every approach I wrote down as a comment, TODO, or "possible route"? If not, go back and attempt them.
2. Are there other open `sorry`s in my file I could attempt with the tools and lemmas I just developed?
3. Did I call the informal agent (or document that no key was available) before filing "Infrastructure missing"?
4. Is there any approach I thought of but skipped because "it would take too long" or "the planner should decide"? If it's in my file's scope, attempt it.

Only proceed to write the task result when all four answers are "yes" or "not applicable."

Before writing results:

1. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with current result, lemmas discovered, concrete next step, dead-end warnings.
2. Save all changes; ensure the file compiles.
3. **Write a `## Summary` section** stating: sorry count before → after; exact names of sorries closed; exact names of sorries still open and why; whether you attempted adjacent sorries beyond the assigned ones.
4. **Write a `## Why I stopped` section** — be brutally honest; the planner reads this. **Only Lean code changes count as progress** — a sorry closed, a helper lemma that compiles, a partial tactic block at a specific stuck point. Comments, docstrings, TODO notes, and task result prose do NOT count. If you wrote approaches in comments that you didn't attempt, say so explicitly:
   - `Real progress`: closed N sorries — name each one. State the sorry count before and after.
   - `Partial progress`: made measurable code progress (decomposed into sub-lemmas with proof bodies, closed one branch of a case split) but did not fully close. Name the specific advance and the specific remaining blocker. Decomposing a `sorry` into N named `sorry`s with only comments in their bodies is NOT partial progress unless the bodies contain genuine proof attempts.
   - `Cosmetics only`: changed formatting/comments/style with no proof progress — say so explicitly. **Renaming a `sorry` to a named helper lemma, adding a docstring, or extracting it to a separate declaration with a `sorry` body is `Cosmetics only` unless the body contains a genuine proof attempt.**
   - `Approaches written but not attempted`: you identified routes (in comments, TODOs, or log prose) but did not attempt them — name each one and why you stopped before trying. This is a valid but weak verdict; the planner will re-assign with a directive to attempt them.
   - `Avoided the goal`: attempted something adjacent but not the assigned target — explain why and what you tried instead.
   - `Infrastructure missing`: a specific Mathlib gap was the blocker — name it precisely, confirm the informal agent was called (or key unavailable), describe what it suggested, why it failed, and state the exact missing ingredient statement.
   - `Directive not followed`: explain which part of the planner's directive you deviated from and why.
